title: Matelea umbellata
intro: Matelea umbellata, also known as the "butterfly vine" or "dual-twister," is a species of flowering plant in the dogbane family (Apocynaceae) native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including pain, inflammation, and fever, due to its bioactive constituents, which include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins. The bioactive constituents of Matelea umbellata have been shown to exhibit analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, contributing to its potential health benefits. The plant is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to alleviate pain, reduce inflammation, and promote relaxation.
taxonomy: Matelea umbellata is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which includes approximately 2,000 species of plants commonly known as dogbanes or oleanders. This family is part of the larger order Gentianales, which encompasses various flowering plant families. Matelea umbellata is further classified within the subfamily Rauvolfioideae, which is a group of flowering plants known for their unique characteristics and adaptations. The genus Matelea is also part of this subfamily and is native to the Americas, with species ranging from Mexico to Argentina. Matelea umbellata's classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants within the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Matelea umbellata is known for its traditional medicinal uses in various cultures. It has been employed to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations. The plant's properties are also believed to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial effects, making it useful for wound healing. Furthermore, Matelea umbellata is thought to have antiseptic and antifungal properties, which may contribute to its use in treating infections. However, more research is needed to fully understand its medicinal properties and potential uses.

The plant has been studied for its possible effects on the cardiovascular system, with some research suggesting that it may help lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels.
